weather: Today in Edmonds: Partly sunny to mostly sunny during the day, becoming partly cloudy tonight. High 65-67°F, low 50-52°F. Light north-northwest winds 5-9 mph. Only a 2-9% chance of rain—excellent May Day weather for any outdoor plans. Evening stays comfortable with clearing skies and light winds becoming calm after midnight.

local_news.findings: Edmonds City Council unanimously adopted the city's first-ever Comprehensive Safety Action Plan on April 28, 2026. The plan identifies 11 priority traffic safety projects citywide that could be eligible for future grant funding, with goals of eliminating traffic fatalities and serious injuries by 2040. The council also approved placing a $383,456 amendment to a consultant contract for phase two of a flow reduction study in the Perrinville drainage basin on a future consent agenda.

The City of Edmonds has shared the 60% design plan for Stage 3 of the Highway 99 Revitalization Project, which covers the 2.3-mile stretch between 244th and 238th Streets Southwest. Construction will begin mid-2028 and complete late 2029 at a cost of $35 million. The public can weigh in on urban design features like public art, softscape, and lighting through an online survey open until May 7.

Edmonds Police Chief Loi Dawkins updated the City Council on efforts to meet state requirements for accessing $210,000 in public safety sales tax revenue collected since February. The department must enhance policies in six areas including use of force, firearms relinquishment, and immigration compliance by June 30 deadline.

The Edmonds City Council received annual reports from the city's prosecutor and public defender offices during their April 28 meeting. Prosecutor Eric Walls reported hiring an additional attorney to address case backlogs, while public defender Kathleen Kyle noted misdemeanor theft remains the top charge in Edmonds with a median theft value of $37.66.

South County Fire will hold a public meeting May 5 on a $420 million bond resolution to rebuild and renovate Edmonds fire stations. The bond would fund reconstruction of aging fire facilities to better serve the community's emergency response needs.

family.choir: Seattle Pro Musica has concluded its 2025-26 auditions, but invites interested singers to email auditions@seattlepromusica.org to join the 2026-27 season mailing list. Auditions for all voice parts are held each August at Epiphany Parish Seattle (1805 38th Ave, Seattle). The Seattle Choral Company is scheduling 2025-26 season auditions by appointment through July and August at Phinney Ridge Lutheran Church; repertoire includes Christmas in Latvia and Baroque masterworks. Choral Arts NW hosts a Music Educator Sing-in on March 15, 2026. Northwest Chorale's current season features Haydn's Creation.
family.toddler: May toddler programs at Sno-Isle Libraries: Edmonds Library offers Toddler Storytime with Cami on May 7 (9:00-9:45am). Lynnwood Library hosts Toddler Storytime with Nathalie on May 4 (9:30-10:00am) and Baby Storytime with Heather on May 1 (11:00am). Imagine Children's Museum in Everett invites toddlers to create bee/butterfly headbands and paint a flower mural on May 9 (10am-12pm). All library storytimes include songs, rhymes and early-literacy activities for ages 18 months-3 years.

intellectual_journals.findings: In a 4,000-word essay published April 24, Flora Champy defends Marcel Proust’s view that literature is not a moral tonic but a technology for personal freedom. Using Proust’s 1905 preface to Ruskin’s Sesame and Lilies as her hinge, Champy argues that books do not make us “better” in any utilitarian sense; instead they preserve fugitive sensations and let us inhabit other minds without the distortions of social speech. The piece is a sustained brief against the instrumental view of reading—against the idea that novels must “pay” in empathy or career advancement—and ends with a Proustian credo:  [TRUNCATED]

A sweeping 7,500-word investigation published April 14 finds that more than 300 U.S. citizens were arrested during Trump-era immigration sweeps on charges such as “assaulting federal officers” or “conspiracy,” yet over one-third of those cases have already collapsed. ProPublica and FRONTLINE reviewed body-cam and bystander video showing that officers’ sworn statements were repeatedly contradicted by the footage; prosecutors quietly dismissed charges or lost at trial. The report documents a chilling effect: even acquitted protesters say they now hesitate to demonstrate, while federal agencies c [TRUNCATED]

Maria Popova’s 6,200-word portrait of Frances “Fanny” Wright (1795-1852), published February 27, excavates the forgotten Scottish-American radical who inspired young Walt Whitman and pioneered scientific materialism, abolitionism, and free love. Drawing on Wright’s own A Few Days in Athens and her Nashoba commune experiment, the essay frames her as a prototype of the “large-orbit” intellect—too expansive for her contemporaries yet foundational to American democratic thought. Popova links Wright’s Epicurean ethics to modern questions of bodily autonomy and collective liberation, arguing that he [TRUNCATED]

In the April 23 issue, Francine Prose reviews two new novels—Lee Clay Johnson’s Bloodline and Carl Hiaasen’s Fever Beach—under the rubric “Blood in the Game.” Prose argues that both writers manage to address the grotesque violence and corruption of the present moment without surrendering the pleasures of fiction. Johnson’s tale of a self-amputating grifter who claims Confederate lineage becomes, in Prose’s reading, a dark satire of heritage mythology and toxic nostalgia, while Hiaasen’s eco-thriller about Florida land scams channels rage at environmental destruction into propulsive comedy. The [TRUNCATED]

wearable_ai.findings: Friend AI pendant (friend.com) is a $99 wearable companion that listens continuously and texts responses to your phone. The 2-inch pendant pairs via Bluetooth and stores memories locally with encryption. After shipping 5,000 units since July 2025, founder Avi Schiffmann reports users treat it as a "living journal"—one rider uses it to plan motorcycle routes, another revived a childhood game-dev ho [TRUNCATED]

District Knowledge syncs curriculum docs so lesson plans auto-align  [TRUNCATED]

CES 2026 revived the AI-pendant category after Humane’s flop. Lenovo/Motorola’s glossy pearl-like pendant (Project Maxwell) and Bee’s $49.99 Pioneer wrist/clip/ pendant lead a wave of lifelogging wearables. Bee records up to 7 days on a charge, supports 40 languages, and stores encrypted audio locally. Meta acquired Limitless; Amazon bought Bee. Chinese startup iBuddi showed a medallion to fight s [TRUNCATED]

triadic_ontology.findings: Two major developments advance the triadic-ontology program. Damiano Migliorini’s Relational Ontologies and Trinitarian Metaphysics: Subsistences, Events and Gunk (Karl Alber, 2025) constructs a rigorous analytic model in which the divine persons are conceived as “subsisting relations” rather than substances, using trinitarian doctrine as an ontological key to resolve the aporia between divine omniscience and human freedom while avoiding the drift toward monism or pantheism he finds in process models. The same publisher launches the new series Studies on Triadic Ontology and Trinitarian Philosophy with Trinitarian Ontologies: Towards a Trinitarian Transformation of Philosophy, ed. Eduard Fiedler & Pavel Frývaldský (2025), a 425-page collection that positions triadic thought as a bridge between analytic and continental camps and traces its roots from late-antique theological transformation to contemporary participatory anthropology. Christopher Isabelle’s Triadic Relational Ontology (ph [TRUNCATED]

ai_systems.findings: Three 2026 papers push autonomous research from lab demos to hardened production systems. InternAgent-1.5 (arXiv:2602.08990) introduces a unified agentic framework for long-horizon scientific discovery, coordinating generation, verification, and evolution subsystems across computational and wet-lab domains. MARS (arXiv:2602.02660) tackles the cost and opacity of AI research by combining budget-aware Monte-Carlo planning, modular code construction, and reflective memory, achieving SOTA on MLE-Bench while exhibiting “Aha!” cross-branch transfer 63 % of the time. Mimosa (arXiv:2603.28986) goes further, evolving multi-agent workflows on-the-fly via a meta-orchestrator and Model Context Protocol tool discovery, hitting 43.1 % success on ScienceAgentBench and exposing heterogeneous model responses to decomposition. Prompt engineering for these reasoning models has also shifted: new guidance from SurePrompts (Apr 2026) shows o3, Claude-extended-thinking, and Gemini Deep Think reward concise g [TRUNCATED]

uap.findings: Rep. Anna Paulina Luna’s House Oversight Task Force is actively investigating UAP disclosure, having held hearings in September 2025 and continuing into 2026. Luna claims to have seen materials “of non-human origin” in a SCIF and is pressing the Pentagon for 46 withheld UAP videos; she has vowed a public press conference once they are declassified. The probes focus on alleged suppression by the All-Domain Anomaly Resolution Office and potential threats to restricted military airspace.

newyorker.title: Reverend Billy Takes On Norman Foster’s New Monolith
newyorker.dek: Fresh from opening shows for Neil Young, the street preacher Billy Talen has moved on from burning Mickey Mouse in effigy to protesting JPMorgan Chase’s ties to fossil fuels, Jake Offenhartz writes.
newyorker.text:
A little after midnight on Earth Day, the street preacher Billy Talen dragged his pulpit to the new Norman Foster-designed global headquarters of JPMorgan Chase, on Madison. His self-ordained mission: deliver a twenty-four-hour sermon, calling attention to the bank’s ties to the fossil-fuel industry. Talen, who is seventy-five, held an umbrella above his white leisure suit and silver bouffant, eying a pair of bank security guards. “Blessings upon your souls, gentlemen,” he bellowed. Members of his activist ensemble, the Stop Shopping Choir, were setting up a live stream in the rain. Savitri Durkee, who directs both the choir and Talen, who is her husband, clipped a mike to his lapel and checked her watch. “It’s go-time, Billy,” she said. “See you on the other side.”
Talen, who goes by Reverend Billy, is fond of what he calls “extreme preaching.” He began in the mid-nineties, marching around Times Square with “Antichrist” Mickey Mouse nailed to a wooden cross, in protest of the neighborhood’s creeping corporatism. In the years since, his focus has shifted to the climate crisis. Often, this involves staging musical protests in hostile venues—banks, pipeline-construction zones—until he and his flock are arrested for trespassing. But, Talen said, even that has begun to feel “predictable.” He elaborated, speaking into a camera: “The only way we’re going to start an effective movement is if each of us goes to the limit of our capabilities and claims our eccentricities. I’m a preacher, so I’m going to preach until I drop.” A choir member checked the stream, and noted that they’d just jumped from five to fourteen viewers. “Earthalujah!” Talen cried.
After decades of sneaking up on bankers and lobbyists, Reverend Billy and the choir recently booked a straight gig: touring the country as Neil Young’s opening act. (Typical song: “A Tree Is the Opposite of a Cop.”) The rock star’s fans seemed receptive to the message, relatively. “After a lifetime of being too strange, people are starting to get us,” Talen said. He’s also the religion editor for the Times-Contrarian, Young’s anti-corporate “digital newspaper.” “Neil is a hands-on publisher,” Talen said. “Sometimes he lets me write these really long things. Other times, he’ll say to me, ‘Billy, what do you mean here? What do you want to leave us with?,’ and we’ll e-mail back and forth on that.” Talen said that he’s learned a lot from Young as a performer: “He’s taught me to hold the rhythm, hit the rhyme. He gets what appeals to a mass audience.” Talen and Durkee were connected with the singer by Young’s wife, Daryl Hannah, whom they met at a 2009 protest against mountaintop-removal coal mining in West Virginia. Talen was later arrested, after depositing mounds of “sacred dirt”—taken from the mountain, he claimed—at two Chase branches in the East Village. The bank has since decreased financing for companies involved in mountaintop-removal mining.
By 8 A.M., dark circles had formed under Talen’s steely blue eyes. “I just need to stay hydrated,” he said. Behind him, a gardener was washing a living-wall installation titled “A Parallel Nature,” designed for Chase by Maya Lin. “I’m finding myself mesmerized by this strange, sculpted nature wall,” Talen said. “It feels like a memory.” After conferring with a guard, the gardener pointed his hose at the protesters in the plaza. Talen pulled a rain slicker over his suit and glowered. Thousands of workers were now streaming into the building, mostly ignoring the doomsday preacher.
Around lunchtime, roughly two dozen choir members and other activists arrived for a 5K run around the bank. Among them was Ahmed, an asylum seeker from Guinea who had taken refuge in the choir’s former headquarters, a vacant bank on Avenue C, after arriving in the United States. “It’s very scary-looking,” he said of Chase’s sixty-story tower. Many of the runners had procured thrift-store blazers or top hats, an effort to blend in with the bankers. Durkee, who wore a pin-striped suit, led the group in some stretches, followed by twelve laps around the block. A construction worker sanding down a railing offered support—“You got this!”—but appeared confused by a runner’s sign accusing Chase of ecocide. “I thought it was, like, a charity thing,” he said.
As night fell, Talen huddled under a blanket with other choir members. His once thunderous voice had dropped to a rasp as he condemned the “gangsters in suits” responsible for rising global temperatures. In the final minutes of his sermon, he found himself at an unusual loss for words. “It feels like, well, extreme exhaustion, I guess,” he said. “Or a long hike that got out of hand, and now I have to come down the mountain.” Had the suffering been worth it? “I’m not sure we cracked the culture,” he said. “But we had a vision, and we did what we could.” ♦

literary_pick.title: A Little Life
literary_pick.author: Hanya Yanagihara
literary_pick.summary: A devastating epic following four college friends—Jude, Willem, JB, and Malcolm—whose lives unfold over decades in New York City. The novel centers on Jude St. Francis, a brilliant litigator whose horrific childhood trauma and self-harm form the emotional core of this 720-page masterpiece. Through its exploration of friendship, trauma, art, and the limits of human endurance, the book has become a contemporary classic, frequently cited by critics and readers as one of the most important novels of the 21st century. Its unflinching examination of suffering and the redemptive power of love has spa [TRUNCATED]

enriched_articles.title: Blood in the Game | Francine Prose | The New York Review of Books
enriched_articles.text:

Reviewed:

by Lee Clay Johnson

Panamerica, 376 pp., $25.00 (paper)

by Carl Hiaasen

Vintage Crime/Black Lizard, 368 pp., $19.00 (paper)

How can a novelist address our current moment without sacrificing the pleasures of fiction? Lee Clay Johnson’s Bloodline and Carl Hiaasen’s Fever Beach answer that question in different, equally impressive ways. Both novels are informed by the grotesque and infuriating nature

enriched_articles.title: The role of literature as the key to personal freedom
enriched_articles.text:
# The role of literature as the key to personal freedom | Aeon Essays

Published: 2026-04-24T10:00:00+00:00

## Summary

Marcel Proust, the author of his novel, In Search of Lost Time (1905), has defended the importance of literature as a key to personal freedom and its ability to influence the lives of individuals. He argues that books are not always the instruments of moral betterment, but they can provide valuable insights into our lives. This comes after John Ruskin argued that books were no

enriched_articles.title: Mass Arrests by Trump's ICE, CBP Repeatedly Fail Under Scrutiny — ProPublica
enriched_articles.text: In a hectic scene, three officers with guns, helmets and camouflage gear are a blur of motion on a sidewalk, and all three are moving in different directions. Behind and beside them are people in street clothes, including one person who has fallen on the sidewalk. They are holding a phone and an agent is standing over them. This story is part of a collaborative investigation from ProPublica and FRONTLINE that includes a documentary, “Caught in the Crackdown,” which premieres April 14 at 10 p.m.

enriched_articles.text: Kremlin says Russian forces will stay in Mali ‘to fight extremism’ | 101.9 Jack FM. # Kremlin says Russian forces will stay in Mali ‘to fight extremism’. MOSCOW, April 30 (Reuters) – The Kremlin said on Thursday that Russian forces would stay in Mali to help the country’s military-led government battle insurgents following a surprise offensive by West Africa’s al Qaeda affiliate and a Tuareg-dominated separatist group.
